raw_text:
John Van Sickle, The Design of Virgii's Bucoiics. (Roma 1978).
Hardie's review suggests that it is a useful exercise to rethink E. 8, 6-13
(with Bowersock) as an address to Octavian, 'though Ajax's sponge sticks in
the throat (276). Van Sickle's study provides a fresh synthesis of numerical
pattern, style, genre, theme, and character and argues that an evolutionary
poetics is the basis of design. 'The plan and the theoretical import of Virgil's
book were perceived as something new and a challenge inpoetics.' (10). Van
Sickle approaches his ideal of "well-tempered criticism" and his arguments
for 'a highly artful and unified whole' require critical and respectful attention
by scholars.

Brown argues that Damoetas' riddle (E. 3. 104-5) depends on the elements of

Nisbet notices Eastern elements (oracles, Jewish eschatology) inE. 4 which
V. adapts to Western modes of thought.
Buchheit underscores Dionysiac
mystery elements inE. 5. Evrard studies the organization and metrics of E. 5.
Lee regards verses 1-2, 48-49, and 54-5 as key lines inE. 5, contrasted with
vv. 13-4. Like E. 3, V's Fifth Eclogue is a dramatic poem or mime with two
subtly
dramatic
characters, Menalcas and Mopsus, who exemplify
rivalry
between poets. Lee's analytical reading is remarkably illuminating. Currie is
concerned with Aegle, Naiadum pulcherrima in E. 6. He argues for the influence of an Italian version of the Midas-Silenus legend (involving Egeria,
Numa, Picus Martius, and Faunus) as reported
by Valerius Antias (fr.6.
Peter) in Arnobius. Egeria of the Italic tale becomes Aegle inVergil's 'capture-and-demand' incident, a recurrent motif. Lieberg suggests that Phoebo
meditante (82) suggests that V. was strongly influenced by the music of the
spheres under the tutelage of Apollo. Tarrant prefers Asinius Pollio as addressee inE. 8; geographical accuracy (relating to the Timavus) is outweighed
by the poet's anticipation. Timavus may even be designed to recall Antenor's
progress from Troy to Padua, an heroic association which would affect the
poet's audience. Bowersock's response reiterates, emphatically, his preference
for Octavian over Asinius Pollio as addressee.

Gagé examines the multiple associations of Tyrrhus, Latinus' shepherd and
father of Silvia. Tyrrhus seems to be a remnant of the Tyrrhei, from Epirus
and Acarnania, confused with the Tyrrhenoi (Etruscans) after their protohistoric
migrations. Scuderi accents Aeneas'
Etruscan associations and ascribes V's tencency in this
regard
to deep-seated philoestruscan attitudes
during the Augustan age.

besetting problems inthe epic. Tiber is the instrument of change of fortune, a
change which forecasts the future and foretokens eventual success. Two Appendices list appearances of Tiber in7-12 and examine V's words for water.

interested in legendary and cult matters than in
topography and existing monuments. Préaux brings the somewhat contentious finds at Lavinium into the context of the prelude to the epic. Tilly
explores the Ager Capenas (A. 7. 695-7) and Lucus Feroniae. She concludes
that V's mention of the Cápenos lucos 'was inconcert with the revivifying of
ancient Italic cults so dear to Augustus, with the existence of the annual fair
and the wide repute of Feronia ...
the colony was already established and
living in his time.' (53) Ferdinando Castagnoli treats the Campi Phlegraei in
expert fashion, with emphasis on earlier accounts and illustrators (Sangallo,
Paoli et al.). Air photographs, revised maps, and succinct background commentary shed light on the Portus Julius, Puteoli, Baiae, Misenum, and Cumae.

M. L. Clarke, 'Poets and Patronsat Rome,' G&R 25 (1978) 46-54.
Both
Buchheit and Clarke
are concerned
with amicitia and patronage.
Buchheit's canvas is more extensive and perhaps less critical of the institution
than
Clarke's who
regrets poets' dependence, hyperbole, and prostitution
sometimes exceeding the conventions. When Augustus, as a new constellation
in the zodiac, requires accommodation by Scorpio, Clarke regards the Vergilian conceit as fertile invention but as prostitution of judgment!

Crowther, inter alia, suggests that Vergil may be a cantor. O'Sullivan detects structural and verbal echoes between Odyssey 9, 526ff and Aeneid 9,
481.
Pearce shows that V. is indebted to Theocritus for the conventional
behaviour attaching to gifts. Eclogue 2, 31-44, reveals the various aspects of
the theme extended and is the culminating example of the practice. Schlunk
deals with the Dolonia (Iliad 10) and the Nisus-Euryalus episode (A. 9) to
show how V.
used and considered ancient commentaries in adapting the
Homeric episode to his Night Episode. There are links between Asios (Iliad)
and Nisus (Aeneid): both are paradigms of death through disobedience and
self-confidence. Tracy detects Cat. 101 and 46 inAeneid 6. 333-6. The double borrowing reflects conflicting emotions inAeneas:
the finality of death
and loss, and the joyful return of spring. Aen. 6. 692 reveals another reference to Cat.
101 in a book closely associated with death, separation, and
farewell.

J. R. G. Wright, 'How Bad a Poet was Calpurnius Siculus.' PCA 75
(1978) 23-4.
77.43.89.117 on Mon, 27 Apr 2026 18:14:16 UTC
59
D'Espérey treats later versions of the killing of the stag by Ascanius which
appear in different guises responding to changes in taste. The repertoire
ranges from pastoral (Vergil, Ovid), to exotic (Statius, Valerius Flaccus), to
violence and horror (Statius), to Silius Italicus' neo-classicism. Fontaine examines the 'spiritual' aspects of the Bucolics which were read and imitated by
Christians (Jerome, Ambrose, Augustine, Prudentius) intheir writings, and by
mural
artists in catacombs and elsewhere. Opelt's Marius Victorinus "war
Lehrer der Rhetorik, neuplatonischer Philosoph und schliesslich Bekämpfer
der arianischen Häresie'; his writings are frequently charged with Vergilian
reminiscenses.
O'Sullivan accepts that iam belongs to tertia and that the insula (where
Ucalegon dwelt) had more than three storeys; that trepidatur ab means 'the
scare
starts from', and
that Ucalegon (Care-not)
suggested
the parody to
Juvenal. Ultimus ardebit parodies proximus ardet, and u/timus is spatial, not
temporal. Wright assesses Calpurnius Ed. 4, with Vergil, E. 1 and judges that
V. breathed unique life into the conventions, whereas Calpurnius evidences
no sure poetic taste and writes from less emotionally urgent reasons.
173.

Nethercut refers to his earlier article (CO (1976) 51-4) and notes that the
constellation occupied by the Moon on 15 October, 70 ВС was Aries. The
intrusion of Saturn, the so-called 'evil' aspect, into the Full Moon patterns is
common
to Julius Caesar, Cicero, Augustus, Ovid, and Horace, and was a
galvanizing force in their natal charts. Rendic-Miocevic deals with problems
attaching to the place-name Andes.
